text: Module 1: The Foundation – What is AI, ML, and DL? To understand Generative AI, you first need to understand the family tree of computer intelligence: ●​ Artificial Intelligence (AI): The broadest concept. It is the simulation of human intelligence processes by computer systems. ●​ Machine Learning (ML): A subset of AI focused on designing systems that can learn and make decisions or predictions based on data, rather than being explicitly programmed. ●​ Deep Learning (DL): A subset of ML that uses "Neural Networks" with many layers (deep models) to capture complex hierarchical patterns. ●​ Generative AI (GenAI): A subset of Deep Learning. While traditional AI classifies or predicts data, GenAI creates entirely new data (text, images, audio, code) based on patterns it learned from existing data. Module 2: How Machines Learn Machine Learning models generally learn in three ways: 1.​ Supervised Learning: The model is trained on labeled data (e.g., emails already tagged as "Spam" or "Not Spam"). These models can be: ○​ Discriminative: Learns the boundary separating classes (e.g., deciding which side of the line a new email falls on). ○​ Generative: Learns the actual distribution of the data to understand how each class generates content, computing the likelihood of an item belonging to a class. 2.​ Unsupervised Learning: The model identifies patterns and structures in unlabeled data (e.g., grouping emails by topic via clustering). 3.​ Reinforcement Learning: An agent learns by interacting with an environment. It receives a reward for good actions and a penalty for bad ones, updating its policy over time to maximize total rewards (e.g., a self-driving car learning to navigate traffic). Module 3: The Evolution to Transformers Before modern GenAI, Deep Learning relied on older models that had specific limitations: ●​ CNNs (Convolutional Neural Networks): Great for image/video recognition, but they struggle to understand the order of elements or long-range dependencies in text. ●​ RNNs & LSTMs (Recurrent Neural Networks): Designed for sequential data (like text). However, as sequences get longer, they suffer from "vanishing gradient problems" (they forget earlier parts of the text). The Solution: Transformers In 2017, a revolutionary paper titled

understand the order of elements or long-range dependencies in text. ●​ RNNs & LSTMs (Recurrent Neural Networks): Designed for sequential data (like text). However, as sequences get longer, they suffer from "vanishing gradient problems" (they forget earlier parts of the text). The Solution: Transformers In 2017, a revolutionary paper titled "Attention Is All You Need" introduced the Transformer architecture. Transformers solved the issues of CNNs and RNNs by using a self-attention mechanism. ●​ How it works: When the model processes a word, "attention" allows it to look at words at other positions in the sequence to understand context (e.g., knowing that "loves" associates with "Alice" in the sentence "Alice loves going to the park"). ●​ Transformers use an Encoder-Decoder architecture. The encoder understands the input, and the decoder generates the output. Module 4: The Titans of GenAI – LLMs, GANs, and Diffusion Generative AI uses several different architectures depending on the type of media it is trying to create: 1. Large Language Models (LLMs) & Autoregressive Models LLMs are massive language models trained on massive amounts of text to understand and generate human-like language. They generate text by predicting the very next "token" (a chunk of words or punctuation) based on probabilities. ●​ BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers): Created by Google in 2018 (340 Million parameters). It uses an Encoder-only design. It reads text from both directions simultaneously to deeply understand context. It is great for tasks like classifying text or extracting entities. ●​ GPT (Generative Pre-Trained Transformer): Created by OpenAI. It uses a Decoder-only design that focuses on generating output text based on context. It generates text sequentially, token by token. ○​ GPT-1 (2018): 117 Million parameters. ○​ GPT-2 (2019): 1.5 Billion parameters. Proved models could do zero-shot tasks (learning without explicit fine-tuning). ○​ GPT-3 (2020): 175 Billion parameters. Proved that simply making the model massive improved performance drastically. ○​ GPT-4 (2023): A "multimodal" model, meaning it can take both text and images as inputs. It was fine-tuned using RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback) to make it safer and more useful. 2. GANs (Generative Adversarial Networks) While

parameters. Proved that simply making the model massive improved performance drastically. ○​ GPT-4 (2023): A "multimodal" model, meaning it can take both text and images as inputs. It was fine-tuned using RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback) to make it safer and more useful. 2. GANs (Generative Adversarial Networks) While LLMs predict text sequentially, GANs generate content in data space (like realistic images). A GAN pits two networks against each other: ●​ The Generator (The Forger): Tries to create fake images from random noise. ●​ The Discriminator (The Detective): Looks at both real images and the forger's images and tries to spot the fakes. ●​ The Result: Through this back-and-forth competition, the generator becomes incredibly good at creating highly realistic synthetic images. 3. Diffusion Models Used for text-to-image generation (like Stable Diffusion or Midjourney). ●​ They work by taking an image and adding "noise" to it until it is pure static (forward diffusion). ●​ The model then learns the reverse process: starting with pure random noise and gradually denoising it to reveal a coherent, high-quality image based on a text prompt. 4. VAEs (Variational Autoencoders) These encode data into a compressed "latent space" and decode it back. They are great for controllable generation and blending different styles. Module 5: Prompting, Fine-Tuning, and RAG How do we make these powerful models do exactly what we want? ●​ Prompting: Guiding the model by giving it questions, instructions, or examples. Advanced techniques include Chain of Thought (CoT) and Tree of Thought (ToT). ●​ Fine-Tuning: Taking a pre-trained model and training it further on a specific dataset (like medical records or legal documents) so it becomes an expert in that domain. Techniques include LoRA, QLoRA, and Instruction Fine-Tuning. ●​ RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation): To stop models from hallucinating (making things up), RAG connects the AI to a database. When you ask a question, it searches the database for accurate documents, adds those facts to your prompt, and then generates a factual response. ●​ Agents: Autonomous AI components that use LLMs to reason, plan, and execute chains of tasks, like querying databases or controlling APIs. When you ask a question, it searches the database for accurate documents, adds those facts to your prompt, and then generates a factual response. ●​ Agents: Autonomous AI components that use LLMs to reason, plan, and execute chains of tasks, like querying databases or controlling APIs. Module 6: Practical Applications and Projects GenAI is transforming almost every industry. Here are some of the practical use cases and project ideas you can build with this knowledge: ●​ Text & NLP: Translating languages, summarizing long articles, and classifying sentiment (positive/negative). ●​ Conversational AI: Building chatbots for basic conversation or Medical QA systems that answer healthcare questions. ●​ Education: Creating Automated Essay Graders. ●​ Security: Detecting Fake News by classifying articles as real or fabricated. ●​ Optimization: Using tools like Ner4Opt to extract math and optimization variables from natural language text. Module 7: Challenges and Ethics Generative AI is not perfect. It faces several severe challenges: ●​ Hallucinations: The model confidently generating false information. ●​ Bias & Fairness: The model reflecting human prejudices found in its training data. ●​ Data Privacy & Security: Risks of inputting sensitive data that might be used for future training. ●​ Prompt Injection: Hackers tricking the AI into ignoring its safety guidelines. ●​ Academic Integrity Policy: When using AI in academics (like ChatGPT or DALL-E), you must explicitly credit the tool, identify which parts of your work were AI-generated, and briefly summarize why you used it.
